region_of_origin: China (Guangdong); widely consumed in Singapore and Malaysia

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Chinese roasted suckling pig; mapped to broader pork food product class FOODON_03302051.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00GJ (pig meat, roasted) is the closest defensible match; no specific suckling pig sub-entry exists in FoodEx2 as of 2024. Used as closest_parent match with adaptation documented in notes.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Chinese roasted suckling pig as a food concept; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Chinese roasted suckling pig is approximately 150g, comprising a mix of crispy skin and tender meat, as served at a Chinese banquet or restaurant.

nutrient_source.title: Suckling Pig (Roasted) — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record cr_id F06100047 is sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo with confidence 0.95 and review_status reviewed. Core macros and micronutrients are taken as-is from the HPB record. Saturated fat estimated at 11.5g/100g based on USDA FoodData Central NDB 10006 (pork, fresh, composite of cuts, cooked, roasted) as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this entry; documented as adapted for that field only.

gi_source.title: N/A — Negligible carbohydrate food; GI not applicable
gi_source.source_match_notes: Suckling pig contains only 0.24g carbohydrate per 100g. Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates. The University of Sydney GI Database does not test foods with negligible carbohydrate content. glycemic_index is set to null (not 0) per Veritas critical rule for low/zero-carbohydrate foods. GL per 150g serving = effectively 0.0 given near-zero available carbohydrate.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium (515mg/100g) combined with high saturated fat (~11.5g/100g)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit suckling pig to no more than 60–80g per occasion, avoid the crispy skin which concentrates sodium and saturated fat, and refrain from pairing with high-sodium dipping sauces such as hoisin or plum sauce.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Negligible carbohydrate (0.24g/100g) but high fat (32.35g/100g) may worsen insulin resistance
type2_diabetes.guidance: Suckling pig has a negligible glycaemic impact due to its near-zero carbohydrate content, but the high total fat and saturated fat content can impair insulin sensitivity over time; individuals with type 2 diabetes should limit portions to 80–100g, choose lean meat over skin, and avoid sweet dipping sauces.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat (~11.5g/100g) and high sodium (515mg/100g) elevate LDL cholesterol and blood pressure
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Suckling pig is high in saturated fat and sodium, both of which are established cardiovascular risk factors; individuals with cardiovascular disease should treat this as an occasional food, limit portions to 60g, avoid the skin, and ensure the overall meal is low in sodium and saturated fat.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High saturated fat and arachidonic acid content promote pro-inflammatory cytokine activity
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Suckling pig contains high saturated fat (~11.5g/100g) and arachidonic acid from pork, which amplify TNF-alpha and IL-6 inflammatory pathways; individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid or strictly limit consumption, particularly the crispy skin which is richest in saturated fat and advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) from high-heat roasting.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Suckling pig (烤乳猪, Kǎo Rǔ Zhū) is a classic Cantonese roasted dish made from a whole baby pig, typically 2–6 weeks old and weighing 3–6 kg, that has not yet been weaned. The pig is marinated with a blend of salt, five-spice powder, soy sauce, hoisin sauce, garlic, and sugar, then roasted whole at high heat until the skin becomes uniformly crispy, golden-brown, and blistered. The interior meat remains tender, juicy, and mildly flavoured. The dish is a centrepiece of Chinese banquets, wedding feasts, and festive celebrations across Singapore, Malaysia, Hong Kong, and Guangdong province. Nutritionally, suckling pig is energy-dense at 388 kcal per 100g, with high fat (32.35g/100g) and substantial protein (23.94g/100g). Carbohydrate content is negligible at 0.24g/100g, making the glycaemic impact essentially zero. Sodium is elevated at 515mg/100g, primarily from the marinade. Saturated fat is approximately 11.5g/100g, concentrated in the skin. Clinically, suckling pig is unsuitable for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease due to its sodium and saturated fat load. For type 2 diabetes, the negligible carbohydrate content is favourable, but the high fat content warrants portion control. The crispy skin is particularly rich in saturated fat and advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) from high-heat roasting, making it a concern for inflammatory conditions such as rheumatoid arthritis.

Is suckling pig high in sodium?
Is suckling pig suitable for hypertension?

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo (cr_id F06100047, review_status reviewed, confidence 0.95). Saturated fat adapted from USDA FoodData Central pork roasted composite as HPB does not separately report this field; documented as adapted. All other fields completed from approved sources.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, full micronutrient panel, and moisture data for Chinese-style roasted suckling pig are not separately reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry F06100047, and no exact suckling pig match exists in USDA FoodData Central as of 2026; these fields are therefore unavailable from any approved source. dietary_fibre_g is naturally zero as suckling pig is a pure meat dish with no plant-derived fibre; confirmed zero per HPB source and consistent with USDA pork roasted data. Saturated fat of 11.5g/100g is adapted from USDA FoodData Central NDB 10006 (pork, fresh, composite of cuts, cooked, roasted) as the closest approved-source equivalent; HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this entry. Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates (0.24g/100g); gi_category set to not_applicable and glycemic_index set to null per Veritas critical rule for low/zero-carbohydrate foods. FoodOn match is broader class FOODON_03302051 (pork food product); no exact FoodOn term for Chinese roasted suckling pig exists. FoodEx2 code A00GJ (pig meat, roasted) is the closest defensible match used as closest_parent; no specific suckling pig sub-entry exists in FoodEx2 as of 2024.
